narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 30, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and Year 3 deciles average 5.91, giving context to how your profile stacks up. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's higher than the 3.24 average among those who passed, and it significantly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to succeed on the bar. In contrast, your low Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest negative pull, pushing the model firmly toward predicting failure because it signals underperformance relative to peers who typically rank much higher. Your LSAT score also worked against you to some degree, contributing toward the failure prediction since it's below the levels seen in most successful bar takers. While your middle income background aligns with over a third of passers and full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the key academic concerns. Overall, your profile shows strengths like a competitive undergrad record but gaps in later school performance and test scores that mirrored patterns in students who struggled on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your high school decile through additional coursework or programs, and gathering letters highlighting your undergrad success and resilience. Many applicants in similar situations improve their profiles and reapply successfully—reach out to admissions for specific feedback or retake strategies tailored to your full-time goals. With targeted steps, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time.
